title: American Fluffy Pancakes

subs.name: 2 tbsp thick curd (dahi) per egg for an eggless version; texture will be slightly denser
buttermilk (chaas/mor) for tangier, fluffier pancakes; add an extra pinch of baking soda
subs.notes: buttermilk reacts with soda for more lift

ingredients.name: melted butter, plus extra for cooking

subs.name: any neutral cooking oil such as sunflower or groundnut oil

steps.text: Whisk together the maida, baking powder, baking soda, sugar and salt in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre.

Beat the eggs in a separate bowl, then stir in the milk, 2 tbsp melted butter and vanilla. Pour this mixture into the well and stir gently until just combined — a few lumps are fine and overmixing will make the pancakes tough.
Rest the batter for 5 minutes. Meanwhile heat a non-stick frying pan or flat tawa on medium-low flame. Brush the surface lightly with the remaining butter.
Pour approximately a quarter-cup of batter onto the pan. Cook undisturbed until the edges look set and bubbles form and pop across the surface, about 2–3 minutes.
Flip once with a flat spatula and cook for a further 1–2 minutes until the underside is golden and the pancake feels slightly springy when pressed at the centre — that is the doneness cue.
Transfer to a warm plate, re-butter the pan, and repeat with the remaining batter. Serve the stack immediately with honey, jam or fresh banana slices.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: milk → buttermilk (chaas/mor) for tangier, fluffier pancakes; add an extra pinch of baking soda (buttermilk reacts with soda for more lift); butter → any neutral cooking oil such as sunflower or groundnut oil; egg → 2 tbsp thick curd (dahi) per egg for an eggless version; texture will be slightly denser
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook on a thick iron dosa tawa over medium-low flame — the heavy base gives even heat without hot spots. Amul butter works perfectly. Maple syrup can be swapped for honey (available at any kirana) or even condensed milk (Milkmaid) for a desi finish.
